chunks.text: Having worked for thirty years as the vice branch manager of a bank
I retired at the mandatory age.
On my last day, my younger colleagues gave me a bouquet and a folded letter.
The letter said, " Thank you for the things you reminded us of.
" I had no dignity of a bishop
but I had urged sincerity in my subordinates.
After retirement
my wife and I set off on an around - the - world trading trip on a luxury cruise ship.
A length of three months at sea.
The ship traveled the Mediterranean, the African coast, and the Mexican Gulf.
At each port we bought item.
But even luxurious days grow tiring.
Lavish meals, dinners in suit and dress, music of a high pitch.
A pretty similar pattern continued every day.
My wife and I, moreover, gained more time to talk.
It was time to rebuild the slow relation we had not been able to have in our working years.
Our son's birthday, our daughter's wedding, the story of how we rear our grandchildren.
We would log our memories while also talking about plans for the future.
The guests on the ship, as an observer would see, were varied.
Retired bankers, writers, lawyers.
Everyone had walked a different life.
We talked with an elderly couple.
The husband was seventy - six.
" Life is a continuation of etc," he laughed.
Life has stages.
Youth, maturity, old age.
Each stage has its own unique beauty.
I want to live the rest of my life with gratitude.
